Notes: R_Programming- Graphics

Graphics
​​​​​​​R provides extensive capabilities for creating both simple and advanced graphics. Starting with basic functions and moving to packages like ggplot2 and plotly, you can create a wide variety of visualizations.
Base Graphics

R is a powerful tool for creating a wide range of graphics and visualizations. Here is a brief guide to creating graphics in R, covering some basic concepts and functions.

Basic Plotting

R comes with a variety of built-in plotting functions. The most basic function is plot(), which can be used for a variety of plots.

# Basic scatter plot
x <- rnorm(100)
y <- rnorm(100)
plot(x, y)

​​​

Customizing Plots

You can customize your plots by adding titles, labels, and changing the color or type of points and lines.

# Custom scatter plot
plot(x, y,
     main = "Scatter plot of X vs Y",
     xlab = "X-axis label",
     ylab = "Y-axis label",
     col = "blue",
     pch = 19)  # pch changes the point type

​

Adding Elements

You can add elements like lines, points, or text to existing plots.

# Adding elements to the plot
abline(h = 0, col = "red")  # Add a horizontal line at y = 0
abline(v = 0, col = "green")  # Add a vertical line at x = 0
text(0, 0, "Center", pos = 4, col = "purple")  # Add text at (0,0)
​​

Multiple Plots

You can create multiple plots in one figure using par(mfrow = c(nrows, ncols)).

# Multiple plots in one figure
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(x, y)
hist(x)
boxplot(x)
plot(density(x))
​​

ggplot2
ggplot2 is a powerful and flexible R package for creating graphics, based on the Grammar of Graphics. This system allows you to build plots layer by layer, making it easier to customize and combine different visual elements.
Installation and Loading

If you haven't already installed ggplot2, you can do so with: 

install.packages("ggplot2")
Then, load the package with:
library(ggplot2)

Basic Structure

The basic structure of a ggplot2 plot involves:

  1. Data: The data frame containing your variables.
  2. Aesthetic Mappings: Mapping variables to visual properties (aesthetics).
  3. Geometries: Geometric objects (geoms) representing data points.

Creating a Basic Plot

Simple scatter plot:

# Sample data
df <- data.frame(x = rnorm(100), y = rnorm(100))

# Basic scatter plot
ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) + 
  geom_point()		​

Advanced Graphics with ggplot2
For more advanced and customizable graphics, the ggplot2 package is widely used. It uses a different approach to plotting called the Grammar of Graphics.
# Installing and loading ggplot2
install.packages("ggplot2")
library(ggplot2)

# Basic ggplot2 scatter plot
df <- data.frame(x, y)
ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) + 
  geom_point()

​

Customizing ggplot2

You can customize ggplot2 plots extensively.

# Custom ggplot2 scatter plot
ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) + 
  geom_point(color = "blue", size = 3) + 
  ggtitle("Scatter plot of X vs Y") + 
  xlab("X-axis label") + 
  ylab("Y-axis label")

​

You can also map aesthetics to variables:

# Sample data with additional column
df <- data.frame(x = rnorm(100), y = rnorm(100), z = rnorm(100))

# Color points by variable z
ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y, color = z)) + 
  geom_point()		​

Combining Plots with ggplot2

You can combine multiple layers and plots using ggplot2.

# Combining layers in ggplot2
ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) + 
  geom_point(color = "blue") + 
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "red") + 
  ggtitle("Scatter plot with regression line")


​

Adding Layers

Layers can be added to plots to represent additional information:

# Adding a smoothing line
ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) + 
  geom_point() + 
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "red")

​

Saving Plots

You can save your plots to a file using functions like png(), jpeg(), or pdf().

# Saving a plot to a file
png("scatter_plot.png")
plot(x, y)
dev.off()

Faceting

Faceting allows you to create multiple plots based on the values of a factor variable:

# Sample data with a factor variable
df <- data.frame(x = rnorm(100), y = rnorm(100), category = rep(c("A", "B"), 50))

# Facet by category
ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) + 
  geom_point() + 
  facet_wrap(~ category)

​

Themes

Themes control the overall look of your plot. You can use built-in themes or create your own.

# Using a built-in theme
ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) + 
  geom_point() + 
  theme_minimal()

# Customizing themes
ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) + 
  geom_point() + 
  theme(
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "lightblue"),
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "white"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 14)
  )		​
